collisions.paper: Chanin et al. (2024). A is for Absorption: Studying Feature Splitting and Absorption in Sparse Autoencoders. arXiv:2409.14507
collisions.overlap: Established absorption as failure mode; proved absorption is logical consequence of sparsity loss (Proposition 2). Did not compare to random baselines or investigate metric calibration.

collisions.paper: Korznikov et al. (2025). OrtSAE: Orthogonal Sparse Autoencoders Uncover Atomic Features. arXiv:2509.22033
collisions.overlap: Reduced absorption by 65% via decoder orthogonality. Compared to standard trained SAEs but not to random baselines.

collisions.paper: Sanity Checks for Sparse Autoencoders (2026). arXiv:2602.14111
collisions.overlap: Frozen/random baselines achieve comparable performance to trained SAEs on general metrics. Does NOT focus on absorption metrics specifically.
collisions.note: Closest prior art but does not address absorption metrics. Our extension to absorption-specific metrics is non-trivial.

collisions.paper: Wang et al. (2026). Does Higher Interpretability Imply Better Utility? ICLR 2026. arXiv:2510.03659
collisions.overlap: Weak correlation (~0.3) between interpretability and steering utility. Consistent with our null results on absorption impact.

collisions.paper: Bussmann et al. (2025). Matryoshka SAEs. ICML 2025
collisions.overlap: Reduced absorption from 0.49 to 0.05 via nested architecture. Architectural solution to absorption.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Primary novelty: first demonstration that trained SAEs have lower absorption than random baselines on the SPECIFIC Chanin differential correlation absorption metric (mean 0.034 vs 0.278, p<0.001). The Sanity Checks paper covers random baselines on general SAE metrics but NOT absorption metrics. This specificity makes our extension non-trivial. Optimal compression reframing builds on Chanin et al.'s Proposition 2 but extends with empirical evidence from H7 (training dynamics).
Hypothesized decoder correlation graph predicts absorption pairs. Falsified: precision@20 = 0.0, Fisher p = 1.0. No novelty assessment needed.

collisions.paper: Sanity Checks for Sparse Autoencoders (2026). arXiv:2602.14111
collisions.overlap: Covers random baseline comparisons at high level; methodology similar to our approach.

candidates.differentiation_notes: Pure null-result paper with methodological contributions. Limited novelty as standalone research direction. Workshop-only visibility likely. Recommend using as fallback only if cand_g framing is rejected.

key_findings: cand_g's core novelty (trained < random absorption on specific Chanin differential correlation metric) is genuinely novel
The Sanity Checks paper (arXiv:2602.14111) is the closest prior art but does NOT focus on absorption metrics specifically—our extension is non-trivial
Chanin et al. provides theoretical foundation (Proposition 2) but does not diminish our empirical contribution
No exact_match found: no prior work demonstrates trained SAEs have lower absorption than random baselines on the Chanin differential correlation metric
Optimal compression reframing builds on Chanin Proposition 2 but extends with training dynamics evidence (H7: trained=0.034 vs random=0.278, p<0.001)
risk_factors: Field skepticism about SAE utility may depress interest regardless of novelty
The optimal compression theory is partially derivative of Chanin et al.'s Proposition 2
Sanity Checks paper (arXiv:2602.14111) is close enough that reviewers may conflate the two works—must address directly in introduction
recommended_citations: Chanin et al. (2024) - Foundational work on absorption definition and Proposition 2; arXiv:2409.14507
Sanity Checks (2026) - Must address directly; our work extends by focusing on absorption metrics specifically; arXiv:2602.14111
Wang et al. ICLR 2026 - Consistent with null results; supports our methodology; arXiv:2510.03659
Korznikov et al. OrtSAE - Architectural context; not in direct competition; arXiv:2509.22033
Bussmann et al. Matryoshka SAEs - Architectural context; ICML 2025
assessment_notes: Novelty assessment confirmed at 7/10 for cand_g. Web search unavailable (API errors); prior art derived from proposal.md citations and existing novelty report. No exact_match found. Sanity Checks paper (arXiv:2602.14111) identified as closest prior but does NOT address absorption metrics specifically, making our contribution genuinely novel.
novelty_score_rationale: Score of 7/10 because: (1) trained < random absorption comparison on the SPECIFIC Chanin differential correlation metric is genuinely novel—the Sanity Checks paper does not address absorption metrics; (2) optimal compression reframing builds on Chanin's Proposition 2 but is not fully original; (3) honest null-result methodology is valuable but not theoretically novel; (4) single-model (GPT-2 Small) limits generalizability claims.
